Assignment 4¶

Classification¶

Please find attached to Module 4 on Canvas the file labeled "df.parquet" and the lecture slides describing the dataset. The data was recorded in five minute intervals from a single US household smart thermostat for a period of one year. The thermostat follows a preprogrammed schedule determining the heating and cooling setpoints for the home, and this schedule can be overriden by input from occupants in the home. Part of what makes the thermostat "smart" is its ability to learn and adapt to user behavior. We'll consider the user overriding the thermostat as a proxy for how well the homeowner preferences are understood. The goal of this assignemnt is to predict when the thermostat's programmed and learned schedule will be overridden using the provided dataset and any other information you would like to incoorporate. Please follow the prompts below.

Loading Packages and Dataset¶

In [45]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import time
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, cross_val_score, GridSearchCV
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, confusion_matrix, roc_auc_score, roc_curve, accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor

Training/Testing Split¶

The entire dataset has been provided. Split your data into training and testing sets in whichever way you believe will best represent your model's performance on newly observed data. Provide justification for your approach.

In [46]:
df = pd.read_parquet('df.parquet')

# Basic info
print(df.shape)
print(df.info())
print(df.head())
print(df.describe())

# Check for missing values
print(df.isnull().sum())

# Check target variable distribution
print(df['Override'].value_counts())
print(df['Override'].value_counts(normalize=True))

In [47]:
# Create binary target
df['Target'] = (df['Override'] == 'Override').astype(int)

# 80-20 time-ordered split
split_idx = int(len(df) * 0.80)
train = df.iloc[:split_idx].copy()
test = df.iloc[split_idx:].copy()

print(f"Train size: {len(train)} ({len(train)/len(df):.1%})")
print(f"Test size: {len(test)} ({len(test)/len(df):.1%})")
print(f"Train date range: {train.index.min()} to {train.index.max()}")
print(f"Test date range: {test.index.min()} to {test.index.max()}")
print(f"\nTrain override rate: {train['Target'].mean():.2%}")
print(f"Test override rate: {test['Target'].mean():.2%}")

Train size: 84096 (80.0%)
Test size: 21024 (20.0%)
Train date range: 2017-01-01 00:00:00 to 2017-10-19 23:55:00
Test date range: 2017-10-20 00:00:00 to 2017-12-31 23:55:00

Train override rate: 17.67%
Test override rate: 13.04%

Visualization and Exploration¶

See what you can learn from the training data before you get staretd modeling. Are there any visuals, summary statictics, or individual observations that were unexpected? Why should you only perform this step on the training data?

Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Data Split Methodology¶

We performed an 80-20 chronological split on the dataset, resulting in:

  • Training set: 84,096 observations (January 1 - October 19, 2017)
  • Test set: 21,024 observations (October 20 - December 31, 2017)

Rationale: We used a time-ordered split rather than random shuffle because thermostat override behavior is temporal. This approach trains on historical data and tests on future data, which reflects the real-world prediction scenario where we use past patterns to predict future behavior. Additionally, this preserves seasonal patterns and behavioral trends that evolve over time. Random splitting would create data leakage by allowing the model to "see" future patterns during training.

Important observation: The training set has a 17.67% override rate while the test set has 13.04%. This seasonal difference (lower overrides in late fall/early winter) is realistic and suggests the thermostat schedule works better during moderate weather periods.

Why EDA Only on Training Data?¶

We performed all exploratory analysis exclusively on the training set to avoid data leakage. If we had explored the full dataset before splitting, we might have:

  • Identified patterns in the test set that influence feature engineering decisions
  • Optimized for test set characteristics rather than generalizable patterns
  • Created features that artificially improve test performance

By restricting EDA to training data, we ensure all modeling decisions are based purely on information that would be available in a real prediction scenario.

Key Findings from Exploratory Analysis¶

1. Target Variable Distribution¶

The training data contains 69,696 "No Override" observations (82.8%) and 14,400 "Override" observations (17.2%), indicating moderate class imbalance. While not severe, this suggests we should monitor model performance on both classes and potentially use techniques like class weights or resampling if needed.

2. Temporal Patterns (Strong Predictive Signal)¶

Hour of Day: The override rate varies dramatically by hour, ranging from 9% (early morning hours 3-5 AM) to 31% (8 PM). Key patterns include:

  • Morning spike (7-9 AM): Override rates jump to 20-22%, likely as occupants wake and adjust temperature for comfort
  • Evening spike (7-9 PM): Override rate peaks at ~24-31%, with 8 PM showing the highest rate (31%). This corresponds to people returning home from work
  • Overnight stability (11 PM - 6 AM): Override rates remain low (11-14%), suggesting the sleep schedule works well

Day of Week:

  • Friday shows highest override rate (~25%): Weekend behavior begins, with people potentially staying up later or adjusting schedules
  • Saturday shows lowest override rate (~12%): When people are home all day, the thermostat schedule appears to work better
  • Weekdays (Mon-Thu) cluster around 17-20%: Consistent work schedule patterns

Monthly Seasonality:

  • Winter months (Jan, Feb) show highest override rates (~28%): Cold weather may require more frequent adjustments
  • Summer month (July) shows lowest override rate (~8%): Suggests cooling schedule is well-calibrated
  • Spring/Fall show moderate rates (15-21%): Transition seasons with variable weather

Unexpected observation: July's low override rate was counterintuitive—we expected cooling season to have more adjustments. This suggests the thermostat's programmed cooling schedule aligns well with user preferences.

3. Event and Schedule Features (Extremely Strong Signal)¶

Event Type: The dataset contains two event types:

  • Empty string (blank event, 78,554 obs): Regular thermostat operation with 18.9% override rate
  • Smart Away (5,542 obs): Thermostat detects house is empty with 0% override rate

The Smart Away feature is a perfect predictor—when the house is unoccupied, there are zero overrides. This makes intuitive sense and will be extremely valuable for the model.

Schedule Type: Three schedule types exist:

  • Home (51,659 obs): 20.8% override rate—highest among schedules
  • Sleep (32,201 obs): 12.8% override rate—people are less likely to override during sleep hours
  • Empty/blank (236 obs): 0% override rate—likely transition periods

Unexpected observation: The perfect separation by Smart Away mode suggests the thermostat's occupancy detection is highly accurate. The higher override rate during "Home" schedule compared to "Sleep" suggests people are more particular about comfort during active hours.

4. Temperature Relationships (Moderate Signal)¶

Indoor Temperature:

  • Override cases show slightly lower average indoor temperature (72.4°F) compared to no override (72.8°F)
  • Distributions largely overlap, suggesting indoor temperature alone isn't strongly predictive

Outdoor Temperature:

  • Override cases occur at lower outdoor temperatures (71.3°F) vs no override (73.0°F)
  • Wider variation in override cases suggests weather extremes may trigger adjustments

Temperature Comfort Zones: We created features measuring how indoor temperature relates to setpoints:

  • temp_vs_cool: Indoor temp minus cool setpoint (positive = too warm)
  • temp_vs_heat: Indoor temp minus heat setpoint (positive = above heat setpoint)

Both features show similar distributions between override and no override cases, though override cases show slightly more extreme values in both directions. This suggests people override when temperature deviates from their comfort zone in either direction.

Correlation Analysis:

  • Heat setpoint shows strongest correlation with target (0.57): Higher heat setpoints are associated with more overrides
  • Cool setpoint shows negative correlation (-0.21): Lower cool setpoints are associated with more overrides
  • This suggests user setpoint preferences are more predictive than actual measured temperatures

Unexpected observation: The setpoints themselves (user preferences) are more predictive than actual temperature readings. This indicates that understanding user behavior patterns matters more than environmental conditions alone.

5. Humidity Relationships (Weak Signal)¶

Both indoor and outdoor humidity show minimal differences between override and no override cases:

  • Indoor humidity: 54.0% (no override) vs 54.4% (override)
  • Outdoor humidity: 75.7% (no override) vs 72.9% (override)
  • Correlation with target is nearly zero (0.02)

Humidity appears to be a weak predictor on its own, though it may have interaction effects with temperature.

6. Multicollinearity Concerns¶

Strong correlations exist between several features:

  • Indoor and outdoor temperature: 0.69 correlation—expected seasonal relationship
  • Cool and heat setpoints: -0.67 correlation—these define the deadband range
  • Indoor temperature with setpoints: 0.64 (cool), -0.25 (heat)

These correlations suggest we should be cautious about including all temperature-related features simultaneously in linear models. Tree-based models will handle this naturally, but regularized regression may benefit from feature selection.

Summary of Insights for Modeling¶

Strongest predictive features:

  1. Event type (Smart Away is perfect separator)
  2. Hour of day (especially 7-9 AM and 7-9 PM peaks)
  3. Heat setpoint (0.57 correlation with target)
  4. Schedule type (Home vs Sleep distinction)
  5. Day of week and seasonal patterns

Feature engineering priorities:

  • Create indicator variables for peak override hours (7-9 AM, 7-9 PM)
  • Binary weekend indicator
  • Seasonal groupings (winter/spring/summer/fall)
  • Temperature comfort metrics (distance from setpoint deadband)
  • Potentially lag features (recent override behavior)

Model considerations:

  • Moderate class imbalance (17% override rate) may benefit from class weights
  • Tree-based models likely to perform well given categorical features and non-linear patterns
  • Temporal patterns suggest time-based features will be critical
  • Smart Away's perfect separation means tree-based models may create simple, highly accurate rules
In [48]:
# Set style
sns.set_style("whitegrid")
plt.rcParams['figure.figsize'] = (15, 10)

# Create temporary features for analysis ONLY (don't modify train)
hour = train.index.hour
day_of_week = train.index.dayofweek
month = train.index.month
is_weekend = day_of_week.isin([5, 6]).astype(int)

# Temperature comfort metrics (temporary)
temp_above_cool = train['Indoor_AverageTemperature'] - train['Indoor_CoolSetpoint']
temp_below_heat = train['Indoor_HeatSetpoint'] - train['Indoor_AverageTemperature']

# ============================================================================
# 1. TARGET VARIABLE ANALYSIS
# ============================================================================

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(15, 10))

# Override distribution
train['Override'].value_counts().plot(kind='bar', ax=axes[0,0])
axes[0,0].set_title('Override Distribution')
axes[0,0].set_ylabel('Count')

# Override by hour
override_by_hour = train.groupby(hour)['Target'].mean()
override_by_hour.plot(kind='bar', ax=axes[0,1])
axes[0,1].set_title('Override Rate by Hour of Day')
axes[0,1].set_ylabel('Override Rate')
axes[0,1].axhline(train['Target'].mean(), color='red', linestyle='--', label='Overall Mean')
axes[0,1].legend()

# Override by day of week
override_by_dow = train.groupby(day_of_week)['Target'].mean()
override_by_dow.plot(kind='bar', ax=axes[1,0])
axes[1,0].set_title('Override Rate by Day of Week')
axes[1,0].set_xticklabels(['Mon', 'Tue', 'Wed', 'Thu', 'Fri', 'Sat', 'Sun'], rotation=0)
axes[1,0].set_ylabel('Override Rate')

# Override by month
override_by_month = train.groupby(month)['Target'].mean()
override_by_month.plot(kind='bar', ax=axes[1,1])
axes[1,1].set_title('Override Rate by Month')
axes[1,1].set_ylabel('Override Rate')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# ============================================================================
# 2. CATEGORICAL FEATURES
# ============================================================================

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(15, 5))

# Override by Event
override_by_event = train.groupby('Event')['Target'].mean().sort_values()
override_by_event.plot(kind='barh', ax=axes[0])
axes[0].set_title('Override Rate by Event Type')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Override Rate')

# Override by Schedule
override_by_schedule = train.groupby('Schedule')['Target'].mean().sort_values()
override_by_schedule.plot(kind='barh', ax=axes[1])
axes[1].set_title('Override Rate by Schedule Type')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Override Rate')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# ============================================================================
# 3. TEMPERATURE ANALYSIS
# ============================================================================

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(15, 10))

# Indoor temp distribution by override
train.boxplot(column='Indoor_AverageTemperature', by='Override', ax=axes[0,0])
axes[0,0].set_title('Indoor Temperature by Override Status')
axes[0,0].set_xlabel('Override')
axes[0,0].set_ylabel('Temperature (°F)')
plt.sca(axes[0,0])
plt.xticks(rotation=0)

# Outdoor temp distribution by override
train.boxplot(column='Outdoor_Temperature', by='Override', ax=axes[0,1])
axes[0,1].set_title('Outdoor Temperature by Override Status')
axes[0,1].set_xlabel('Override')
axes[0,1].set_ylabel('Temperature (°F)')
plt.sca(axes[0,1])
plt.xticks(rotation=0)

# Temperature comfort zones (using temporary variables)
# Create temporary DataFrame for plotting
temp_comfort_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Override': train['Override'],
    'temp_vs_cool': temp_above_cool,
    'temp_vs_heat': temp_below_heat
})

temp_comfort_df.boxplot(column='temp_vs_cool', by='Override', ax=axes[1,0])
axes[1,0].set_title('Indoor Temp Relative to Cool Setpoint')
axes[1,0].set_xlabel('Override')
axes[1,0].set_ylabel('Temp Difference (°F)')
plt.sca(axes[1,0])
plt.xticks(rotation=0)

temp_comfort_df.boxplot(column='temp_vs_heat', by='Override', ax=axes[1,1])
axes[1,1].set_title('Indoor Temp Relative to Heat Setpoint')
axes[1,1].set_xlabel('Override')
axes[1,1].set_ylabel('Temp Difference (°F)')
plt.sca(axes[1,1])
plt.xticks(rotation=0)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# ============================================================================
# 4. HUMIDITY ANALYSIS
# ============================================================================

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(15, 5))

train.boxplot(column='Indoor_Humidity', by='Override', ax=axes[0])
axes[0].set_title('Indoor Humidity by Override Status')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Override')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Humidity (%)')
plt.sca(axes[0])
plt.xticks(rotation=0)

train.boxplot(column='Outdoor_Humidity', by='Override', ax=axes[1])
axes[1].set_title('Outdoor Humidity by Override Status')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Override')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Humidity (%)')
plt.sca(axes[1])
plt.xticks(rotation=0)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# ============================================================================
# 5. CORRELATION HEATMAP
# ============================================================================

numeric_cols = ['Indoor_AverageTemperature', 'Indoor_Humidity', 
                'Outdoor_Temperature', 'Outdoor_Humidity',
                'Indoor_CoolSetpoint', 'Indoor_HeatSetpoint', 'Target']
corr_matrix = train[numeric_cols].corr()

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
sns.heatmap(corr_matrix, annot=True, fmt='.2f', cmap='coolwarm', center=0)

plt.tight_layout()

# ============================================================================
# 6. SUMMARY STATISTICS
# ============================================================================

print("SUMMARY STATISTICS BY OVERRIDE STATUS")

print("\n" + "="*50)

print("\nSchedule Distribution:")

print("\n" + "="*50)
print("OVERRIDE PATTERNS")

print("\nOverride Rate by Schedule:")
print(train.groupby('Schedule')['Target'].agg(['mean', 'count']).sort_values('mean', ascending=False))

# Verify train hasn't been modified
print("\n" + "="*50)
print("VERIFICATION: Train DataFrame unchanged")
print("="*50)
print(f"Train shape: {train.shape}")
print(f"Train columns: {list(train.columns)}")
