------------------------------------------------------------
✓ Grid search complete

============================================================
BEST HYPERPARAMETERS FOUND
============================================================
  class_weight: balanced
  max_depth: None
  max_features: sqrt
  min_samples_leaf: 2
  min_samples_split: 2
  n_estimators: 100

  Cross-validation ROC-AUC: 0.9796

------------------------------------------------------------
TOP 5 PARAMETER COMBINATIONS
------------------------------------------------------------

Rank 1:
  Params: {'class_weight': 'balanced', 'max_depth': None, 'max_features': 'sqrt', 'min_samples_leaf': 2, 'min_samples_split': 2, 'n_estimators': 100}
  Mean ROC-AUC: 0.9796 (±0.0167)

Rank 2:
  Params: {'class_weight': 'balanced', 'max_depth': None, 'max_features': 'log2', 'min_samples_leaf': 1, 'min_samples_split': 2, 'n_estimators': 100}
  Mean ROC-AUC: 0.9794 (±0.0166)

Rank 3:
  Params: {'class_weight': 'balanced', 'max_depth': 10, 'max_features': 'log2', 'min_samples_leaf': 2, 'min_samples_split': 2, 'n_estimators': 100}
  Mean ROC-AUC: 0.9791 (±0.0165)

Rank 4:
  Params: {'class_weight': 'balanced', 'max_depth': 20, 'max_features': 'sqrt', 'min_samples_leaf': 2, 'min_samples_split': 2, 'n_estimators': 100}
  Mean ROC-AUC: 0.9790 (±0.0169)

Rank 5:
  Params: {'class_weight': 'balanced', 'max_depth': 20, 'max_features': 'sqrt', 'min_samples_leaf': 2, 'min_samples_split': 2, 'n_estimators': 200}
  Mean ROC-AUC: 0.9783 (±0.0181)

============================================================
STEP 4: TRAIN FINAL MODEL
============================================================

Final Model Configuration:
  Algorithm:            Random Forest Classifier
  Original features:    43
  Encoded features:     48
  Training samples:     84,096
  Positive class rate:  17.67%

  Hyperparameters:
    class_weight: balanced
    max_depth: None
    max_features: sqrt
    min_samples_leaf: 2
    min_samples_split: 2
    n_estimators: 100

✓ Model training complete

============================================================
STEP 5: GENERATE PREDICTIONS
============================================================

Predictions generated:
  Training set: 84,096 predictions
  Test set:     21,024 predictions

Predicted class distribution (Test set):
  No Override: 18,191 (86.52%)
  Override: 2,833 (13.48%)

Actual class distribution (Test set):
  No Override: 18,283 (86.96%)
  Override: 2,741 (13.04%)

============================================================
SECOND MODEL: FITTING COMPLETE
============================================================

Model Summary:
  Approach:          'Kitchen Sink' (all features)
  Model Type:        Random Forest Classifier
  Total features:    48
  Trees in forest:   100
  Max tree depth:    None
  CV ROC-AUC:        0.9796
  Training samples:  84,096
  Test samples:      21,024

Comparison to First Model:
  Model 1: Logistic Regression, 6 features, CV AUC: 0.8541
  Model 2: Random Forest, 48 features, CV AUC: 0.9796

✓ Improvement: +0.1255 AUC

============================================================
Ready for evaluation...
============================================================

Second Model - Evaluation¶

Create an in-sample and out-of-sample ROC curve and calculate the AUC for each.

In [55]:
# ============================================================================
# SECOND MODEL: EVALUATION
# ============================================================================

print("="*60)
print("SECOND MODEL: EVALUATION")
print("="*60)

# ============================================================================
# ROC CURVES AND AUC (In-Sample vs Out-of-Sample)
# ============================================================================

print("\n" + "="*60)
print("ROC CURVES AND AUC SCORES")
print("="*60)

# Calculate ROC curves
fpr_train_v2, tpr_train_v2, _ = roc_curve(y_train, y_train_proba_v2)
fpr_test_v2, tpr_test_v2, _ = roc_curve(y_test, y_test_proba_v2)

# Calculate AUC
auc_train_v2 = roc_auc_score(y_train, y_train_proba_v2)
auc_test_v2 = roc_auc_score(y_test, y_test_proba_v2)

print("\nArea Under the Curve (AUC) Scores:")
print(f"  IN-SAMPLE (Training):     {auc_train_v2:.4f}")
print(f"  OUT-OF-SAMPLE (Test):     {auc_test_v2:.4f}")
print(f"  Difference (Train - Test): {auc_train_v2 - auc_test_v2:.4f}")

# Interpret generalization
diff = abs(auc_train_v2 - auc_test_v2)
if diff < 0.02:
    print("\n✓ Excellent generalization (difference < 0.02)")
elif diff < 0.05:
    print("\n✓ Good generalization (difference < 0.05)")
elif diff < 0.10:
    print("\n⚠️  Slight overfitting (0.05 ≤ difference < 0.10)")
else:
    print("\n⚠️  Significant overfitting (difference ≥ 0.10)")

# Plot ROC curves
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 7))

ax.plot(fpr_train_v2, tpr_train_v2, linewidth=2.5, color='blue',
        label=f'In-Sample (Train) - AUC = {auc_train_v2:.4f}')
ax.plot(fpr_test_v2, tpr_test_v2, linewidth=2.5, color='green',
        label=f'Out-of-Sample (Test) - AUC = {auc_test_v2:.4f}')
ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], 'k--', linewidth=1.5, label='Random Classifier (AUC = 0.50)')

ax.set_xlabel('False Positive Rate', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('True Positive Rate', fontsize=12)
ax.set_title('ROC Curves: In-Sample vs Out-of-Sample Performance\nRandom Forest (Kitchen Sink)', 
             fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax.legend(fontsize=11, loc='lower right')
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# ============================================================================
# PERFORMANCE METRICS
# ============================================================================

print("\n" + "="*60)
print("PERFORMANCE METRICS")
print("="*60)

print("\nIN-SAMPLE (Training Set):")
print(f"  Accuracy:  {accuracy_score(y_train, y_train_pred_v2):.4f}")
print(f"  Precision: {precision_score(y_train, y_train_pred_v2):.4f}")
print(f"  Recall:    {recall_score(y_train, y_train_pred_v2):.4f}")
print(f"  F1-Score:  {f1_score(y_train, y_train_pred_v2):.4f}")
print(f"  ROC-AUC:   {auc_train_v2:.4f}")

print("\nOUT-OF-SAMPLE (Test Set):")
print(f"  Accuracy:  {accuracy_score(y_test, y_test_pred_v2):.4f}")
print(f"  Precision: {precision_score(y_test, y_test_pred_v2, zero_division=0):.4f}")
print(f"  Recall:    {recall_score(y_test, y_test_pred_v2):.4f}")
print(f"  F1-Score:  {f1_score(y_test, y_test_pred_v2, zero_division=0):.4f}")
print(f"  ROC-AUC:   {auc_test_v2:.4f}")

# ============================================================================
# DETAILED CLASSIFICATION REPORT
# ============================================================================

print("\n" + "="*60)
print("DETAILED CLASSIFICATION REPORT (Test Set)")
print("="*60)

print(classification_report(y_test, y_test_pred_v2, 
                           target_names=['No Override', 'Override'],
                           zero_division=0))

# ============================================================================
# CONFUSION MATRICES
# ============================================================================

print("\n" + "="*60)
print("CONFUSION MATRICES")
print("="*60)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(15, 5))

# Training confusion matrix
cm_train_v2 = confusion_matrix(y_train, y_train_pred_v2)
sns.heatmap(cm_train_v2, annot=True, fmt='d', cmap='Blues', ax=axes[0], 
            cbar_kws={'label': 'Count'})
axes[0].set_title('In-Sample (Training) Confusion Matrix', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
axes[0].set_ylabel('True Label', fontsize=11)
axes[0].set_xlabel('Predicted Label', fontsize=11)
axes[0].set_xticklabels(['No Override', 'Override'])
axes[0].set_yticklabels(['No Override', 'Override'])

# Test confusion matrix
cm_test_v2 = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_test_pred_v2)
sns.heatmap(cm_test_v2, annot=True, fmt='d', cmap='Blues', ax=axes[1], 
            cbar_kws={'label': 'Count'})
axes[1].set_title('Out-of-Sample (Test) Confusion Matrix', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
axes[1].set_ylabel('True Label', fontsize=11)
axes[1].set_xlabel('Predicted Label', fontsize=11)
axes[1].set_xticklabels(['No Override', 'Override'])
axes[1].set_yticklabels(['No Override', 'Override'])

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Print confusion matrix breakdown
print("\nConfusion Matrix Breakdown (Test Set):")
tn, fp, fn, tp = cm_test_v2.ravel()
print(f"  True Negatives (TN):  {tn:,} - Correctly predicted No Override")
print(f"  False Positives (FP): {fp:,} - Incorrectly predicted Override")
print(f"  False Negatives (FN): {fn:,} - Missed Override (predicted No Override)")
print(f"  True Positives (TP):  {tp:,} - Correctly predicted Override")

print(f"\nFalse Positive Rate: {fp/(fp+tn):.2%}")
print(f"False Negative Rate: {fn/(fn+tp):.2%}")
print(f"True Positive Rate (Recall): {tp/(tp+fn):.2%}")
print(f"True Negative Rate (Specificity): {tn/(tn+fp):.2%}")

# ============================================================================
# FEATURE IMPORTANCE
# ============================================================================

print("\n" + "="*60)
print("FEATURE IMPORTANCE (TOP 15)")
print("="*60)

# Get feature importance
feature_names_v2 = X_train_encoded.columns
feature_importance_v2 = final_model_v2.feature_importances_

# Create DataFrame
feature_importance_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Feature': feature_names_v2,
    'Importance': feature_importance_v2
}).sort_values('Importance', ascending=False)

print("\nTop 15 Most Important Features:")
print(feature_importance_df.head(15).to_string(index=False))

# Visualize top 15 features
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))
top_15 = feature_importance_df.head(15)
plt.barh(range(len(top_15)), top_15['Importance'], color='steelblue')
plt.yticks(range(len(top_15)), top_15['Feature'])
plt.xlabel('Feature Importance', fontsize=11)
plt.title('Top 15 Feature Importances (Random Forest)\nKitchen Sink Model', 
          fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
plt.gca().invert_yaxis()
plt.grid(axis='x', alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print("\n" + "-"*60)
print("Feature Importance Interpretation:")
print("-"*60)
print("  - Higher importance: Feature is more useful for making predictions")
print("  - Importance is based on how much feature reduces impurity across trees")
print("  - Sum of all importances = 1.0")

# ============================================================================
# MODEL COMPARISON: MODEL 1 vs MODEL 2
# ============================================================================

print("\n" + "="*60)
print("MODEL COMPARISON: MODEL 1 vs MODEL 2")
print("="*60)

comparison_table = pd.DataFrame({
    'Metric': ['Model Type', 'Features', 'CV ROC-AUC', 'Train ROC-AUC', 
               'Test ROC-AUC', 'Test Accuracy', 'Test Precision', 
               'Test Recall', 'Test F1-Score'],
    'Model 1 (Simple)': [
        'Logistic Regression',
        '6 features',
        f'{0.8541:.4f}',
        f'{auc_train:.4f}',
        f'{auc_test:.4f}',
        f'{accuracy_score(y_test, y_test_pred):.4f}',
        f'{precision_score(y_test, y_test_pred, zero_division=0):.4f}',
        f'{recall_score(y_test, y_test_pred):.4f}',
        f'{f1_score(y_test, y_test_pred, zero_division=0):.4f}'
    ],
    'Model 2 (Kitchen Sink)': [
        'Random Forest',
        '48 features',
        f'{best_cv_score:.4f}',
        f'{auc_train_v2:.4f}',
        f'{auc_test_v2:.4f}',
        f'{accuracy_score(y_test, y_test_pred_v2):.4f}',
        f'{precision_score(y_test, y_test_pred_v2, zero_division=0):.4f}',
        f'{recall_score(y_test, y_test_pred_v2):.4f}',
        f'{f1_score(y_test, y_test_pred_v2, zero_division=0):.4f}'
    ]
})

print("\n" + comparison_table.to_string(index=False))

print("\n" + "-"*60)
print("KEY IMPROVEMENTS:")
print("-"*60)
print(f"  CV ROC-AUC:    {best_cv_score - 0.8541:+.4f} ({((best_cv_score - 0.8541) / 0.8541 * 100):+.1f}%)")
print(f"  Test ROC-AUC:  {auc_test_v2 - auc_test:+.4f} ({((auc_test_v2 - auc_test) / auc_test * 100):+.1f}%)")
print(f"  Test Recall:   {recall_score(y_test, y_test_pred_v2) - recall_score(y_test, y_test_pred):+.4f}")
print(f"  Test F1:       {f1_score(y_test, y_test_pred_v2) - f1_score(y_test, y_test_pred):+.4f}")

# ============================================================================
# FINAL SUMMARY
# ============================================================================

print("\n" + "="*60)
print("SECOND MODEL: SUMMARY")
print("="*60)

print("\nModel Characteristics:")
print(f"  Type:              Random Forest (Kitchen Sink)")
print(f"  Complexity:        High (48 features, 100 trees)")
print(f"  Features:          All engineered features included")
print(f"  Training samples:  {len(X_train_encoded):,}")
print(f"  Test samples:      {len(X_test_encoded):,}")

print("\nPerformance Summary:")
print(f"  Cross-Validation ROC-AUC: {best_cv_score:.4f}")
print(f"  In-Sample ROC-AUC:        {auc_train_v2:.4f}")
print(f"  Out-of-Sample ROC-AUC:    {auc_test_v2:.4f}")
print(f"  Test Accuracy:            {accuracy_score(y_test, y_test_pred_v2):.4f}")
print(f"  Test Precision:           {precision_score(y_test, y_test_pred_v2, zero_division=0):.4f}")
print(f"  Test Recall:              {recall_score(y_test, y_test_pred_v2):.4f}")
print(f"  Test F1-Score:            {f1_score(y_test, y_test_pred_v2, zero_division=0):.4f}")

print("\nKey Insights:")
if auc_test_v2 > auc_test:
    print(f"  1. ✓ Significant improvement over Model 1 (+{auc_test_v2 - auc_test:.4f} test AUC)")
else:
    print(f"  1. ⚠️  Lower test AUC than Model 1 ({auc_test_v2 - auc_test:.4f})")

if diff < 0.05:
    print(f"  2. ✓ Good generalization (train-test gap: {diff:.4f})")
else:
    print(f"  2. ⚠️  Some overfitting (train-test gap: {diff:.4f})")

recall_v2 = recall_score(y_test, y_test_pred_v2)
if recall_v2 > 0.5:
    print(f"  3. ✓ Better recall ({recall_v2:.2%}) - catches more overrides")
else:
    print(f"  3. ⚠️  Still low recall ({recall_v2:.2%}) - misses many overrides")

print(f"  4. More balanced predictions (13.48% vs actual 13.04%)")

print("\n" + "="*60)
print("SECOND MODEL EVALUATION COMPLETE")
print("="*60)

------------------------------------------------------------
Feature Importance Interpretation:
------------------------------------------------------------
  - Higher importance: Feature is more useful for making predictions
  - Importance is based on how much feature reduces impurity across trees
  - Sum of all importances = 1.0

============================================================
SECOND MODEL EVALUATION COMPLETE
============================================================

Discussion¶

Which of your two models would you put into produciton and why? For this model, how to you think about the tradeoff between true positive rate and false positive rate, and what do you think is the right balance between these two measures in performance (what weight or cutoff would you use). Did you learn anything from creating the two models? How smart to you think the Ecobee thermostat is based on your results?

Model Selection for Production¶

I would put Model 2 (Random Forest) into production.

Rationale:

Model 2 demonstrates dramatically superior performance across all metrics:

  • Test AUC: 0.9984 vs 0.6945 (Model 1), a 43.8% improvement
  • Test Recall: 99.09% vs 34.51% (Model 1), catches 2,716 of 2,741 actual overrides instead of missing 1,795
  • Test F1-Score: 0.9745 vs 0.5111 (Model 1), nearly doubles the balanced performance metric
  • Generalization: Train-test AUC gap of only 0.0016 shows excellent generalization despite perfect training AUC

While Model 1's simplicity (6 features, easily interpretable) is appealing, its 34.51% recall means it misses nearly two-thirds of override events. In a smart thermostat system, this would lead to poor user experience. The system would consistently fail to anticipate when users need to manually adjust temperature, defeating the purpose of learning user preferences.

Model 2's near-perfect performance (99.09% recall with 95.87% precision) suggests it has genuinely learned the patterns governing user override behavior. The feature importance analysis reveals the model relies heavily on meaningful predictors: deadband width (17.8%), heat setpoint (14.5%), cool setpoint (13.0%), and engineered comfort features. These are interpretable, domain-relevant features that make physical sense for predicting thermal comfort needs.

Practical considerations for production:

  • Computational cost: Random Forest with 100 trees and 48 features is still computationally efficient for a thermostat operating on 5-minute intervals
  • Maintenance: The model requires no real-time scaling or preprocessing
  • Robustness: Tree-based models handle missing data and outliers well
  • Update frequency: Could retrain weekly or monthly as new override patterns emerge

True Positive Rate vs False Positive Rate Trade-off¶

In a smart thermostat context, there are two types of errors:

  • False Positives (FP): System predicts override when user would not actually override

    • Impact: Unnecessary temperature adjustments; slight energy waste; minor comfort disruption
    • User perception: "The thermostat adjusted itself, but I was fine with the previous setting"

  • False Negatives (FN): System fails to predict override that user will make

    • Impact: User must manually override; defeats "smart" learning purpose; user frustration
    • User perception: "This thermostat has not learned my preferences. I still have to adjust it myself"

Current Model 2 Performance:

  • False Positive Rate: 0.64% (117 false alarms out of 18,283 no-overrides)
  • False Negative Rate: 0.91% (25 missed overrides out of 2,741 overrides)
  • True Positive Rate (Recall): 99.09%
  • True Negative Rate (Specificity): 99.36%

My recommendation: Prioritize Recall (minimize False Negatives)

Reasoning:

  1. User experience: Missing an override (FN) is more frustrating than a preemptive adjustment (FP). Users tolerate a system that is "too helpful" better than one that "has not learned anything."

  2. Energy cost: The difference in energy consumption between FP and FN is minimal. Both involve temperature adjustments. A false positive might adjust temperature unnecessarily, but so does a false negative when the user manually overrides anyway.

  3. Trust building: A smart thermostat builds trust by anticipating needs. High recall shows the system "understands" the user. Low recall makes users question whether the "smart" features work at all.

  4. Class imbalance consideration: With only 13.04% of intervals resulting in overrides, even a small false positive rate in absolute terms means relatively few false alarms (117 out of 21,024 predictions).

Proposed threshold adjustment:

Currently using default threshold (0.5). I would lower the threshold to 0.3-0.4 to increase recall further:

# Instead of: predicted = (probabilities > 0.5)
# Use: predicted = (probabilities > 0.35)

Expected outcome:

  • Push recall from 99.09% toward 99.5-99.9% (catch virtually all overrides)
  • Accept slightly higher FP rate (perhaps 1-2% instead of 0.64%)
  • Trade-off: approximately 100 additional false positives to catch approximately 10-20 additional true overrides

Justification: In practice, making 200 unnecessary adjustments across 21,024 intervals (0.95% FPR) to ensure catching 99.8% of actual user needs is a favorable trade-off. Users will perceive the system as highly responsive rather than frustratingly passive.

Alternative approach for production: Implement user-specific threshold tuning:

  • Start with conservative threshold (0.35-0.4) for new users
  • If user consistently reverses predicted adjustments, increase threshold (reduce FP)
  • If user frequently overrides after no prediction, decrease threshold (reduce FN)
  • Personalize the precision-recall balance to individual user tolerance

Lessons Learned from Creating Two Models¶

1. Feature engineering matters enormously

Model 1 used only 6 raw features. Model 2 used 48 features including engineered ones. The most important features in Model 2 were engineered features:

  • deadband_width (CoolSetpoint - HeatSetpoint): 17.8% importance
  • setpoint_midpoint (average of heat/cool setpoints): 12.0% importance
  • temp_below_heat (distance from heat setpoint): 5.2% importance

These engineered features captured thermal comfort relationships that raw temperatures could not express. This demonstrates that domain knowledge applied to feature engineering can be more valuable than algorithm sophistication.

2. The multicollinearity "problem" was not actually a problem

Model 1 required careful VIF analysis and removal of correlated features (Indoor_CoolSetpoint had VIF=342). We removed it despite moderate correlation with target (r=-0.21) to avoid multicollinearity in linear regression.

Model 2 (Random Forest) used all temperature features without issue, achieving vastly better performance. Tree-based models naturally handle correlated features, and the redundant information actually helped capture complex patterns.

Lesson: Do not reflexively remove correlated features. Consider whether your algorithm actually requires feature independence before discarding potentially useful information.

3. Simple models can fail even with strong individual predictors

Model 1's strongest predictor (Indoor_HeatSetpoint, r=0.57) still resulted in poor test performance (AUC=0.6945). The linear model could not capture:

  • Non-linear relationships between temperature and comfort
  • Interactions between time, temperature, and user preferences
  • Threshold effects (e.g., overrides only when temperature exceeds comfort zone by X degrees)

Model 2's tree structure naturally models these complexities, demonstrating that algorithm choice matters as much as feature selection.

4. Temporal distribution shift is a real challenge

Both models showed different performance patterns, but Model 1 was dramatically affected by the temporal split:

  • Model 1: CV AUC=0.8541 to Test AUC=0.6945 (drop of 0.1596)
  • Model 2: CV AUC=0.9796 to Test AUC=0.9984 (actually improved)

The test period (Oct-Dec) had lower override rates (13.04%) than training (17.67%). Model 1 learned seasonal correlations that did not generalize. Model 2 learned more robust patterns (comfort zones, time patterns, schedule types) that transferred across seasons.

Lesson: When facing temporal data, complex models with rich feature sets may actually generalize better than simple models, contrary to traditional wisdom about overfitting.

5. Perfect training performance is not always bad

Model 2's training AUC of 1.0 initially raised red flags. However, test AUC of 0.9984 proved this was not problematic overfitting. This revealed an important insight: some prediction tasks are genuinely very predictable given sufficient information.

The near-perfect performance suggests thermostat override behavior follows strong, learnable patterns rather than being random or chaotic.

6. The "kitchen sink" approach can work

Contrary to the principle of parsimony, throwing in all 48 features (including many engineered ones) dramatically outperformed careful feature selection. With sufficient data (84,096 training samples) and regularization (min_samples_leaf, balanced class weights), Random Forest effectively performed automatic feature selection through importance weighting.

Lesson: Do not over-optimize for simplicity prematurely. Let powerful algorithms handle feature selection automatically, then interpret results.

How Smart is the Ecobee Thermostat?¶

Based on our results: Surprisingly smart for this household, but with important caveats.

Important Limitation: Our analysis is based on data from a single household over one year (2017). Conclusions about the Ecobee's "smartness" are specific to this user's behavior and cannot be generalized to all Ecobee users or other time periods. Different households may have different comfort preferences, occupancy patterns, and override behaviors.

Baseline observation from descriptive statistics: Across the full year of data, this household overrode the thermostat 16.75% of the time (17,603 overrides out of 105,120 five-minute intervals). This translates to approximately 48 manual adjustments per day. This baseline override rate serves as our primary metric for evaluating the thermostat's intelligence. A truly intelligent system would minimize the need for manual intervention.

Evidence of "smartness" (for this household):

  1. Override rate is relatively low (16.75% annually)

For this household in 2017, the 16.75% annual override rate (ranging from 13.04% in the late fall/winter period to 17.67% in the January-October period) suggests the pre-programmed schedule works reasonably well for this particular user's comfort needs. If the thermostat were completely oblivious to this user's needs, override rates would likely be much higher.

  1. Predictable patterns exist for this user

Our Model 2 achieved 99.09% recall on this household's data, meaning override behavior is highly predictable from available features. This suggests this user's comfort needs follow consistent, rational patterns rather than being arbitrary. The thermostat's sensors (temperature, humidity, occupancy) capture the right information to predict this user's needs.

  1. Smart Away works perfectly

The Event="Smart Away" feature had 0% override rate in our data. When the thermostat detects the house is empty, it never gets overridden. This occupancy detection appears highly reliable and represents genuine "smart" functionality for this household.

  1. Schedule-based learning shows promise

Schedule types (Home vs Sleep) had different override rates (20.8% vs 12.8%), and the model heavily weighted these features. This suggests the thermostat's schedule-based approach has merit for this user's routine, though execution could improve.

Evidence of limitations (for this household):

  1. Override rate of 16.75% indicates room for improvement

Even with "smart" features, this user overrode the thermostat approximately 17,603 times over the year. This suggests:

  • The thermostat has not fully learned this individual's comfort preferences
  • Static schedules cannot adapt to dynamic comfort needs
  • Current "learning" algorithms may be rudimentary
  1. Seasonal variation suggests incomplete adaptation

The drop from 17.67% overrides (Jan-Oct) to 13.04% (Oct-Dec) for this household suggests:

  • The thermostat may not adapt its schedule seasonally based on learned patterns
  • Or, moderate fall weather simply requires less adjustment for this user
  • Either way, there is room for improvement in seasonal learning
  1. Strong dependence on user-configured setpoints

The most important features were user-configured setpoints (heat/cool preferences) rather than learned behavior patterns. This suggests the "smart" features are primarily executing this user's explicitly specified preferences rather than discovering preferences through observation.

How "smart" really? (for this single household)

My assessment: 6/10 on the "smart" scale

Scoring Framework:

I am evaluating "smartness" based on how well the thermostat minimizes the need for manual intervention while maintaining comfort. A truly "smart" system would learn and adapt to reduce overrides over time.

Score Breakdown:

What earns points (+6/10):

  • ✓ Occupancy detection (Smart Away): +2 points

    • Perfect 0% override rate when away
    • Demonstrates reliable occupancy sensing

  • ✓ Basic functionality: +2 points

    • Maintains temperature within user-specified ranges
    • Executes programmed schedules consistently
    • Reliable sensor accuracy (temperature, humidity)

  • ✓ Some pattern awareness: +1 point

    • Schedule types (Home vs Sleep) show different override rates (20.8% vs 12.8%)
    • Suggests basic context awareness of user activity states

  • ✓ User preference compliance: +1 point

    • Setpoint configurations strongly influence behavior
    • System respects user-defined comfort boundaries

What loses points (-4/10):

  • ✗ High override rate: -2 points

    • 16.75% annual override rate = approximately 17,600 manual interventions per year
    • That is 48 overrides per day on average
    • A truly "smart" system would reduce this to <5% (<5,000 per year)
    • Current performance: Prevents 83% of potential overrides
    • Expected "smart" performance: Should prevent 95% or more of potential overrides

  • ✗ No temporal pattern learning: -1 point

    • Override rates vary 9% (3AM) to 31% (8PM), yet thermostat does not adapt schedule
    • Friday has 25% override rate vs Saturday 12%, no weekday/weekend schedule differentiation
    • System could preemptively adjust for high-override hours but does not

  • ✗ No apparent individual learning: -1 point

    • Feature importance shows user-configured setpoints matter most, not learned preferences
    • System executes what user explicitly programs, not what it observes
    • A "learning" system would automatically suggest setpoint adjustments based on override patterns

Benchmark for scoring:

Score Description Expected Override Rate
10/10 Fully adaptive AI <2% (learns perfectly, predicts all needs)
8/10 Advanced learning <5% (anticipates most situations)
6/10 Basic automation 15-20% (follows rules, limited learning)
4/10 Simple programmable 25-35% (just executes fixed schedule)
2/10 Manual control 40-50% (frequently needs adjustment)
0/10 Broken/non-functional >50% (constant intervention needed)

Justification for 6/10: The Ecobee for this household performs at the "basic automation" level. The no-override rate of 83.25% suggests the thermostat's schedule is acceptable for most time intervals, though the 16.75% override rate (approximately 17,600 manual adjustments per year) indicates substantial room for improvement. The high reliance on user-configured setpoints rather than learned patterns, combined with the inability to adapt schedules based on observed temporal patterns (hour-of-day, day-of-week effects), suggests limited "learning" capability. The perfect Smart Away performance earns credit, but the significant override frequency indicates room for improvement in anticipating user needs.

Data-driven comparison: Our Model 2, which uses the same sensor data the Ecobee has access to, achieved 99.09% recall (would reduce overrides from 17% to <1%). This demonstrates that the hardware and data are sufficient for much smarter behavior. The limitation is in the thermostat's current algorithms, not the available information.
